title: Melanoma recognition by a deep learning convolutional neural networkdPerformance in different melanoma subtypes and localisations
brief_summary: This study investigated the diagnostic performance of the Moleanalyzer-Pro deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for classifying melanomas and benign lesions across different subtypes and localizations. The CNN was applied to six dermoscopic image sets, each containing 30 melanomas and 100 benign lesions. The primary aim was to determine if the CNN could counterbalance physicians' diagnostic difficulties associated with specific melanoma characteristics, such as advanced thickness in acrolentiginous or nodular subtypes. The research utilized the current market version of the CNN approved for the European market. The study focused on evaluating the system's ability to distinguish between malignant and benign classifications in diverse clinical scenarios. This work addresses the gap in understanding how CNN performance varies across different melanoma subtypes and localizations.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on melanoma recognition, specifically analyzing performance across different melanoma subtypes (e.g., acrolentiginous, nodular) and localizations. Each of the six dermoscopic image sets included 30 melanomas.
Used as the comparator class for malignant/benign classification. Each of the six dermoscopic image sets included 100 benign lesions.

datasets_used.name: Six dermoscopic image sets
datasets_used.description: Six distinct sets of dermoscopic images collected from various clinical centers (University of Heidelberg, Medical University of Graz, Hospices Civils de Lyon, Aristotle University, Konstanz, Hospital Thalkirchner Street, University of Passau, FotoFinder Systems GmbH, University of Goettingen) to evaluate the Moleanalyzer-Pro CNN across different melanoma subtypes and localizations.

important_remarks: The study evaluates the 'current market version' of the Moleanalyzer-Pro CNN approved for the European market, which implies the model architecture and training data are proprietary and not fully disclosed, limiting reproducibility.

The dataset consists of six dermoscopic image sets, each containing exactly 30 melanomas and 100 benign lesions; this small sample size per set (130 images total) may limit the statistical power and generalizability of the performance estimates.

The study design focuses on evaluating performance across different melanoma subtypes and localizations to see if the CNN can counterbalance physician difficulties, but the provided context does not report the specific quantitative results (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, AUC) or statistical significance of these findings.
